source_title: How Alex Hormozi Gets Other People To Build His $100M+ Empire

paragraphs.speaker: alex
paragraphs.name: Alex Hormozi
paragraphs.text: The highest returns on capital we get as entrepreneurs is talent. Like, full stop. Where else do you get 10x, 20x, 100x returns so reliably? If I'm thinking, I really have to fill this role angle, it's usually not the right person. If I'm thinking, I don't even care if I have a role for this person, I have to get them in, it's usually the right person. Once you get to like 5 million in revenue, that's what you have to become as a collector of people.

paragraphs.speaker: sam
paragraphs.name: Sam Parr
paragraphs.text: You think in frameworks. I don't think like that at all. That does not come naturally to me. Whenever I hear you or Ryan Dice, he was making me feel like a jerk. He was like, you don't think in frameworks? Like, how do you learn something and then think how you're going to teach it to your team or whatever? And I'm like, I don't know, man. I'm like a Neanderthal. I just kind of float by life sometimes. I don't reflect and think in a framework. But you do that.

paragraphs.speaker: alex
paragraphs.name: Alex Hormozi
paragraphs.text: I think frameworks happen when you have to reteach or reuse the same thought process over and over again. And so rather than rederiving the same decision set, you create a framework to give yourself mental shorthand. I created the Mosey 6 or whatever—metrics, market, model, money, and then manpower. These are the six reasons that people get limited in their business, right? There is a decision tree that I'm going through. You totally think in frameworks. You just haven't documented them.

paragraphs.speaker: alex
paragraphs.name: Alex Hormozi
paragraphs.text: The management document was like, all right, fundamentally, people don't do stuff for a reason. I have to figure out what that reason is. If there's a way that I can have a framework that I can have for this conversation that makes it less like, how do I not attack the person? Rather than say, you are a lazy piece of shit, it's like, that's unlikely to be productive. Most people do prefer to stay employed and also prefer to do a good job, I think, by and large.

paragraphs.speaker: alex
paragraphs.name: Alex Hormozi
paragraphs.text: If you're hiring for what people consider low skill labor, you're going to hire for attitude, not aptitude. The amount of skills required to train someone on attitude far outweigh the skills it takes to teach them how to use a cash register. On the flip side, if you want to have the number one AI researcher in the world, if that guy's a bit of a dick, then you could probably work with him on being a little bit better at communicating with the team. You're not going to take the yogurt store cashier and make them the number one AI researcher.

paragraphs.speaker: alex
paragraphs.name: Alex Hormozi
paragraphs.text: The biggest change in terms of my hiring practices is I hire far more for general intelligence. The intelligence will allow someone to bridge the skill gap faster. I define intelligence as rate of learning. If I can get someone who has a high rate of learning, maybe that person starts a little bit behind, but in six months, they're going to pass the other person who might have experience.

paragraphs.speaker: sam
paragraphs.name: Sam Parr
paragraphs.text: When you're hiring for smart people, how do you figure out if they're smart?

paragraphs.speaker: alex
paragraphs.name: Alex Hormozi
paragraphs.text: The quality of the questions that they ask. If someone said, hey, I noticed that you guys have this media brand here and you have this advisory practice here. What's revenue retention around that? Is there another vehicle that you guys have considered? I'd be like, wow, that demonstrated that they did a fair amount of research. Versus someone says like, what's the five year vision of the company? That's kind of a generic interview question. A lot of my stuff is public. And so I expect somebody to come in with better than generic questions.

paragraphs.speaker: alex
paragraphs.name: Alex Hormozi
paragraphs.text: At acquisition.com, especially at our higher level leadership roles, instead of presenting hypothetical cases, we actually present them with the real cases. Worst case, we get free consulting. Best case, we get somebody who is capable of building that solution that they just came up with. If they can use frameworks that I wasn't aware of, experiences that I'm not aware of, those things will come out. It's also way more interesting of an interview for me, because now they're solving my problem.

paragraphs.speaker: alex
paragraphs.name: Alex Hormozi
paragraphs.text: The easy answer is settling. But there's trades because these things don't exist outside of time. You have to have somebody that when I'm on the phone with them, I'm thinking I have to have this person. If I'm thinking from the like, I really have to fill this role angle, it's usually not the right person.
